question_text: A crane lifts a steel beam vertically upward. The beam weighs 5000 N. Initially, the tension in the cable is 5000 N and the beam accelerates upward. After a few seconds, the tension becomes 5000 N and the beam moves at constant velocity. What does this change tell us about the forces and motion? Choose the best explanation.

options.label: A
options.text: The tension increased, so the beam accelerated faster.

options.label: B
options.text: The tension stayed the same, so the resultant force remained constant and acceleration continued.

options.label: C
options.text: When tension equals weight, the resultant force is zero, so the beam stops accelerating and moves at constant velocity (equilibrium).

options.label: D
options.text: The beam is no longer in contact with the ground, so friction no longer acts and the forces become unbalanced.

explanation: Initially, tension (5000 N up) exceeds weight (5000 N down), giving a net upward force that causes acceleration. When tension equals weight, the forces balance (resultant = 0 N), so by Newton's first law, the beam stops accelerating and moves at constant velocity. This is the definition of equilibrium.
